does: This paper introduces ForeAgent, an agentic forensics framework for detecting AI-generated images that combines a Perception-Verdict architecture with iterative self-refinement capabilities. The perception module aggregates multi-view forensic cues across semantic, spatial, and frequency domains to detect subtle artifacts left by generative models. A multimodal large language model serves as a verdict module that fuses these diverse signals into logically grounded detection decisions. The framework implements a Hindsight-Driven Self-Refining strategy using a Sampling-Reflection-Evolution paradigm, enabling the agent to iteratively improve its detection performance through inference rollouts without requiring costly retraining on outputs from frontier models.
connects: This work extends recent efforts applying multimodal large language models to forensic analysis tasks, addressing limitations in prior deepfake detection approaches that either lack fine-grained artifact sensitivity or depend on expensive static synthetic supervision from cutting-edge generative models. It builds on established multi-domain forensic analysis techniques—combining frequency-domain, spatial, and semantic features for detecting synthetic content—while leveraging agent-based reasoning frameworks that have emerged in agentic AI systems. The paper responds to the broader challenge of detecting increasingly realistic AI-generated images as generative model capabilities advance, drawing on self-supervised learning and continual improvement paradigms to reduce reliance on labeled synthetic data.
enables: By decoupling detection from static supervision of frontier models, ForeAgent opens pathways for more sustainable, evolving detection systems that adapt as generative capabilities advance. The self-refining agent framework could extend to other forensic domains requiring adaptation to shifting artifact patterns. The multi-view perception architecture provides a foundation for hybrid detection systems combining classical forensic analysis with modern deep learning. The hindsight-driven learning approach suggests directions for agents that improve through reflection on failed cases without human annotation, potentially scaling detection capabilities across diverse image synthesis methods and enabling faster response to emerging generative techniques in adversarial detection scenarios.
